title: Topic 95: The Anchoring Effect in Game Stores: How $100 Packs Make $20 Look Cheap
content:
### Abstract

This paper delves into the murky, treacherous, and often hilarious waters of in-game stores, a digital bazaar where logic goes to die and wallets weep. We explore the psychological phenomenon of anchoring, a cognitive bias that game developers have masterfully weaponized to make ludicrously expensive virtual items seem like a bargain. Through a rigorous, albeit side-splitting, analysis of pricing strategies, we reveal how a $100 microtransaction for a jpeg of a dragon can make a $20 purchase for a slightly different colored jpeg of a sword feel like a fiscally responsible decision. This research serves as a sobering, yet entertaining, reminder that in the world of gaming, the house always wins, and the anchor always holds. We also question whether our years of academic training have been a colossal waste of time, given that we now spend our days analyzing the economics of virtual hats. The answer, we fear, is yes.

### Introduction

The digital marketplace of a modern video game is a strange and wondrous place, a neon-lit bazaar of virtual goods where the normal rules of commerce are suspended. Here, a pixelated sword can cost more than a real one, a virtual pet can command a king's ransom, and the concept of "value" becomes as fluid and elusive as a greased-up otter in a water park. At the heart of this chaotic emporium of digital desire lies a powerful and pervasive psychological tool: the anchoring effect. This cognitive bias, first identified by Tversky and Kahneman in the 1970s, describes our tendency to rely heavily on the first piece of information offered (the "anchor") when making decisions. In the context of game stores, this means that the first price a player sees can dramatically influence their perception of all subsequent prices.

Game developers, being the clever and occasionally diabolical puppet masters that they are, have harnessed the power of anchoring with a finesse that would make a seasoned car salesman blush. They have created a world where a $100 "Super Ultimate Legendary Mega Pack" of virtual cards, a purchase that in any sane context would be considered financial suicide, serves as a brilliant anchor. Suddenly, the $20 "Slightly Less Legendary But Still Pretty Good Pack" seems not just reasonable, but an outright steal. It’s a masterclass in psychological manipulation, a digital sleight of hand that leaves players feeling like savvy consumers, even as they’re being fleeced.

This paper will embark on a perilous journey into the heart of the virtual marketplace, exploring the myriad ways in which the anchoring effect is used to separate gamers from their hard-earned cash. We will examine the psychological underpinnings of this phenomenon, analyze its application in a variety of popular games, and discuss the ethical implications of its use. We will also, in all likelihood, make a number of ill-advised in-game purchases in the name of "research," a sacrifice we are willing to make for the advancement of human knowledge. So, grab your wallets, dear readers, and prepare to be anchored. It’s going to be a bumpy, and expensive, ride.

### Literature Review

The concept of anchoring, while now a cornerstone of marketing in the digital age, has its roots in the hallowed halls of academia, a place where the only microtransactions are the exorbitant fees for journal access. The seminal work on the subject was conducted by the intellectual titans Tversky and Kahneman, who in 1974, first introduced the world to the “anchoring and adjustment” heuristic. Their early experiments were charmingly low-tech, involving nothing more than a roulette wheel and a series of questions about the percentage of African nations in the United Nations. They found that a completely random number from the wheel could significantly influence a person's estimate, a finding that has since been replicated in countless studies and has made us all deeply suspicious of carnival games.

One of the most entertaining, and frankly, disturbing, demonstrations of the anchoring effect comes from the work of Dan Ariely. In one study, he asked students to write down the last two digits of their social security number and then bid on various items. The results were as predictable as they were hilarious: students with higher social security numbers were willing to pay significantly more for the same items. This experiment, which has been replicated with everything from bottles of wine to cordless keyboards, demonstrates the terrifying power of an arbitrary number to anchor our perception of value. It also suggests that if you have a high social security number, you should probably avoid auctions, and possibly, all forms of commerce.

The application of anchoring in marketing is, of course, nothing new. Retailers have long understood the power of a high “original” price to make a sale price seem like a bargain. However, the digital marketplace of video games has taken this principle to a whole new level. As Om Tandon of GameAnalytics points out, the “decoy effect” is a common strategy in game monetization. By presenting players with a series of options, one of which is deliberately less attractive, developers can nudge players towards a more expensive purchase. This is the digital equivalent of putting a dented can of beans next to a pristine one and charging a premium for the latter. It’s a simple, yet brutally effective, tactic that preys on our innate desire to get a “good deal.”

Further research into the psychology of in-game purchases has revealed a host of other cognitive biases that are exploited by game developers. The endowment effect, for example, describes our tendency to overvalue things that we own. This is why games often give players a “taste” of a premium item, knowing that once we’ve had it, we’ll be more likely to pay to keep it. It’s the digital equivalent of a drug dealer giving you the first hit for free, a comparison that is both alarmingly accurate and deeply depressing.

### Analysis

The brilliance of the anchoring effect in game stores lies in its subtlety. It’s a silent, invisible force that shapes our purchasing decisions without us even realizing it. The process begins the moment a player opens the in-game store. They are immediately presented with a dazzling array of options, each with a different price tag. The most prominent of these is almost always the most expensive: the “$99.99 Ultimate Founder’s Pack,” the “Limited Edition Dragon’s Hoard of Gems,” or some other equally absurdly named and priced item. This, dear reader, is the anchor.

This initial, exorbitant price serves as a powerful psychological benchmark. It recalibrates our perception of value, making all subsequent prices seem smaller in comparison. The $19.99 “Starter Pack,” which in any other context might seem like a significant amount of money to spend on a collection of pixels, now appears to be a reasonable, almost frugal, purchase. It’s a classic case of “sticker shock” being used as a weapon. The initial shock of the high price is quickly replaced by a sense of relief when we see the lower prices, a relief that we often mistake for a good deal.

The decoy effect, as mentioned earlier, is a key component of this strategy. Game developers will often include a mid-range option that is deliberately poor value. For example, a player might be presented with the following options:

*   100 Gems for $4.99
*   250 Gems for $9.99
*   1000 Gems for $29.99

In this scenario, the 250-gem pack is the decoy. It’s a better value than the 100-gem pack, but a significantly worse value than the 1000-gem pack. The player, in an attempt to be a rational consumer, will compare the options and conclude that the 1000-gem pack is the “best deal.” They may not have intended to spend $30, but the carefully constructed pricing scheme has led them to believe that it is the most logical choice. It’s a beautiful, and terrifying, example of how our desire to be smart shoppers can be turned against us.

Another key psychological principle at play is the concept of “pain of paying.” This refers to the negative feeling we experience when we part with our money. Game developers have devised a number of ingenious ways to minimize this pain. The use of virtual currencies, such as “gems,” “coins,” or “crystals,” is a classic example. By converting real money into a virtual currency, developers create a psychological distance between the player and their money. Spending 1000 gems feels less painful than spending $30, even though they are functionally equivalent. It’s a simple, yet effective, way to numb the financial senses.

The use of limited-time offers and “flash sales” is another common tactic. These create a sense of urgency, forcing players to make a quick decision before the “deal” disappears. This sense of urgency can override our natural tendency to be cautious with our money, leading to impulsive purchases that we may later regret. It’s the digital equivalent of a high-pressure salesman telling you that “this offer is only good for today!”

### Real-World Examples

To truly appreciate the masterful application of the anchoring effect in video games, one need only look at some of the most popular titles on the market. These games, while often brilliant in their design and execution, are also monuments to the art of psychological manipulation. Let us examine a few case studies, shall we?

**Case Study 1: The FIFA Ultimate Team Pack Extravaganza**

EA Sports' *FIFA* series, and its wildly popular Ultimate Team mode, is a masterclass in anchoring. The store is a constantly rotating carousel of packs, each promising the chance to acquire a legendary player who will lead your team to glory. The most expensive of these packs, often costing the equivalent of $20 or more, serve as the anchor. They are the digital equivalent of a Bugatti in a car showroom, a ludicrously expensive item that makes everything else seem affordable. The player, after scoffing at the price of the “Jumbo Rare Players Pack,” is then presented with a “Premium Gold Pack” for a mere $5. Suddenly, the $5 pack seems like a bargain, a small price to pay for a shot at glory. It’s a strategy that has made Ultimate Team a billion-dollar enterprise, and has left a trail of broken controllers and empty wallets in its wake.

**Case Study 2: The Clash of Clans Gem Bonanza**

Supercell’s *Clash of Clans* is another game that has perfected the art of the anchor. The game’s premium currency, gems, can be used to speed up construction, train troops, and generally make life in the game a whole lot easier. The in-game store presents players with a range of gem packs, from a modest “Pile of Gems” for $4.99 to a ludicrous “Chest of Gems” for $99.99. The “Chest of Gems” is the anchor, a price so high that it makes the smaller packs seem like a steal. The player, who may have initially balked at the idea of spending any money on the game, is now tempted by the “Bag of Gems” for $9.99. It’s a classic bait-and-switch, a digital con game that has been played out millions of times, to the tune of billions of dollars.

**Case Study 3: The Fortnite V-Bucks Vortex**

Epic Games' *Fortnite* is a cultural phenomenon, a game that has captured the hearts and minds of millions of players around the world. It is also a masterclass in monetization. The game’s virtual currency, V-Bucks, can be used to purchase a dizzying array of cosmetic items, from new outfits to ridiculous dance moves. The V-Bucks store is a perfect example of anchoring in action. The largest pack of V-Bucks, 13,500 for $99.99, serves as the anchor. This makes the smaller packs, like the 1,000 V-Bucks for $9.99, seem like a much more reasonable purchase. The player, who desperately wants the new “Llama-Corn” skin, is now much more likely to drop $10 on the game. It’s a strategy that has made *Fortnite* one of the most profitable games in history, and has turned its developers into virtual currency tycoons.

### Discussion

The pervasive use of the anchoring effect in game stores raises a number of important questions, not least of which is, “Are we all just a bunch of easily manipulated sheep?” The answer, it seems, is a resounding “yes.” The success of these monetization strategies is a testament to the power of cognitive biases, and a sobering reminder of our own fallibility. We like to think of ourselves as rational creatures, making carefully considered decisions based on logic and reason. But the truth is, we are often at the mercy of our own minds, easily swayed by a well-placed anchor or a cleverly designed decoy.

The ethical implications of these practices are, of course, a matter of some debate. On the one hand, it can be argued that game developers are simply using standard marketing techniques to sell their products. There is nothing illegal about using the anchoring effect, and it is a practice that is common in many industries. On the other hand, it can be argued that these practices are predatory, designed to exploit the psychological vulnerabilities of players, particularly younger players who may be more susceptible to these tactics. The line between clever marketing and outright manipulation is a fine one, and it is a line that many game developers seem to be dancing on with a reckless abandon.

Ultimately, the responsibility for navigating this treacherous digital landscape lies with the consumer. By understanding the psychological principles at play, we can become more discerning consumers, less likely to be swayed by the siren song of a “good deal.” We can learn to recognize the anchor for what it is: a clever trick designed to part us from our money. And we can, in theory, make more rational purchasing decisions. But let’s be honest, that new “Legendary Dragon” skin is pretty cool, and it’s only $20. What a bargain.

### Conclusion

In the grand casino of in-game stores, the house always has an edge. The anchoring effect, a subtle yet powerful psychological tool, is one of the many ways that game developers ensure that the odds are always in their favor. By presenting players with a high initial price, they can recalibrate our perception of value, making smaller purchases seem like a bargain. It’s a strategy that is as brilliant as it is diabolical, a masterclass in psychological manipulation that has turned the video game industry into a multi-billion dollar behemoth.

As we have seen, the anchoring effect is a pervasive and powerful force, one that is difficult to resist, even when we are aware of its influence. It is a testament to the fact that we are not the rational creatures we like to think we are. We are, in fact, a collection of cognitive biases, a bundle of irrational impulses, all of which can be exploited by a clever marketer with a good understanding of human psychology.

So, the next time you find yourself in an in-game store, tempted by a “limited time offer” or a “special bundle,” take a moment to consider the anchor. Ask yourself if you are really getting a good deal, or if you are simply being played. And then, in all likelihood, buy the damn thing anyway. Because, let’s face it, that virtual hat is pretty sweet.
references:
[1] Tversky, A., & Kahneman, D. (1974). Judgment under Uncertainty: Heuristics and Biases. *Science*, *185*(4157), 1124–1131. https://www.science.org/doi/abs/10.1126/science.185.4157.1124

[2] Ariely, D., Loewenstein, G., & Prelec, D. (2003). "Coherent Arbitrariness": Stable Demand Curves without Stable Preferences. *The Quarterly Journal of Economics*, *118*(1), 73–106. https://academic.oup.com/qje/article-abstract/118/1/73/1867240

[3] Tandon, O. (2017, September 19). *5 Effective Behavioural Economic Monetization Patterns*. GameAnalytics. https://www.gameanalytics.com/blog/5-effective-behavioural-economic-monetization-patterns

[4] Wikipedia. (2024, October 26). *Anchoring (cognitive bias)*. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Anchoring_(cognitive_bias)
